title: Remove artificial sweeteners from your diet

chunks.title: Introduction to Artificial Sweeteners
chunks.summary: The introduction discusses the ongoing debate in the nutrition sphere regarding artificial sweeteners and their effects on metabolism and gut microbiota. The segment highlights key insights from recent research linking sucralose and other sweeteners to insulin resistance.
chunks.transcript: remove artificial sweeteners there's been a lot of back and forth in the nutrition sphere about artificial sweeteners yes they don't have calories but there's some very interesting research both on the way that artificial sweeteners things like Stevia sucralose which is Splenda aspartame can interrupt Quorum sensing which is communication between the microbes and your guts and even more compelling to me is the research from Dana small at Yale University hopefully I'll get her on the podcast soon but I'm going to show an

chunks.title: Research Findings on Sucralose
chunks.summary: This segment delves into specific findings from a 2020 study conducted by Dana Small at Yale, which explored how the addition of sucralose to a carbohydrate drink causes a mismatch leading to rapid insulin resistance in both adult and adolescent humans. The host emphasizes the significance of this research and how it’s often overlooked by proponents of artificial sweeteners.
chunks.transcript: article from her group from 2020 and what happened in this article was that Splenda was added to a drink with dextrose so sucralose a non-coloric sweetener was added to a drink with dextrose which is an oligosaccharide of glucose so pure glucose so there's no fructose in this drink and what you have then when humans consume this drink you have a mismatch between presented nutrients and sweetness and what happened in both adults humans and especially in adolescent humans was that when carbohydrates were presented with artificial sweeteners specifically sucralose in this case people became insulin resistant really fast there were metabolic derangements

chunks.title: Implications of Combining Sucralose with Carbs
chunks.summary: The discussion continues by explaining why consuming sucralose with other carbohydrates can impair metabolic function. The host critiques the marketing of products that combine sucralose with sugar, asserting that such combinations are detrimental to health and metabolism.

chunks.title: Call to Action: Removing Artificial Sweeteners
chunks.summary: The host urges viewers to eliminate artificial sweeteners from their diets, including Stevia and monk fruit, until more research proves they have different effects than sucralose. The segment concludes with a cautionary statement about the potential metabolic disruptions these sweeteners may cause.
chunks.transcript: we've just begun to understand this in the research and no one is being held accountable for this and they're being marketed as lower sugar this is actually a really really important finding something that you should not ignore so let me show you this article title of the paper is short-term consumption of sucralose with but not without carbohydrate impairs neural and metabolic sensitivity to sugar in humans it's from cell metabolism 2020 again the final author is Dana small you can find her YouTube presentation at the neuron conference which talks about this at around minute 36. so I gave you guys a summary of what happened in this if you really want to go read the paper you can see it but this is a metabolic nightmare for humans and no one should ignore this and no one who is saying that artificial sweeteners are benign should be allowed to say that without explaining this finding and explaining how this is somehow benign for humans I think this is horrible for humans so step three remove artificial sweeteners do not mess with this stuff guys and yes this means Stevia and yes this means monk fruit until we have data to show those are any different you are mismatching a sweet taste with a caloric presentation and I believe that is going to cause metabolic arrangement in humans so I don't I've always been suspicious of monk fruit and Stevia for a variety of reasons but I don't think monk fruit is good for humans and I think that this could be causing a Divergence and a derangement in insulin sensitivity if you are eating those foods with presented other carbohydrates there's good data to suggest this at least with sucralose we need to do these studies with stevia aspartame and monk fruit as well to prove that those are not doing the same thing we definitely know this with Splenda which is sucralose again 3 000 Foods out there have sucralose and presented calories like all these drinks and things like this which are
